title: Transaction import + dedup/merge prompt
description:
## Scope

Fetch transactions from Plaid for the linked item, map them to `SpendTransaction`, run the dedup check against existing manual transactions before import, and prompt to merge on a match rather than double-count. This is an explicit MVP requirement per PROJECT_SPEC.md ("This is a Phase 1 (MVP) concern, not deferred — double counting breaks the core mechanic's trust") — not deferrable.

Depends on adq.6.1 (`PlaidService`/token must exist to call Plaid's `/transactions/get` or `/transactions/sync`). Does not depend on adq.6.2 (Sandbox is sufficient to build and test this against).

**Absorbs reservoir-adq.4's scope** (see NOTES — this decision). adq.4 ("MerchantRule auto-tagging") was narrowed on 2026-07-07 to cover only import-time application of the shared `MerchantMatcher` function built in adq.3; that's a single AC-sized piece of this story's import pipeline, not a separable feature. Building it as its own story would mean either a no-op stub dependency or artificial sequencing overhead for a few lines of pipeline wiring. This story's acceptance criteria include applying `MerchantMatcher` to each newly-imported transaction before it's saved — adq.4 is closed as superseded by this story (see NOTES) rather than tracked separately. Flag to JP if this call should be reverted.

## UX

### Import trigger and flow
This story defines the import pipeline itself; adq.6.4 wires it to the actual triggers (foreground, pull-to-refresh). For this story, a debug-only manual trigger (same posture as adq.6.1's temporary entry point) is sufficient to build and test the pipeline.

### Dedup detection
- Before saving any newly-fetched Plaid transaction, check existing `SpendTransaction`s where `entryMethod == .manual` for a match: same date (± same calendar day, `Calendar.current`, matching the app's existing day-boundary convention) + same amount (exact) + same merchant (case-insensitive exact match, consistent with `MerchantMatcher`'s existing matching semantics — no fuzzy matching, per PROJECT_SPEC).
- On a match: **do not auto-import**. Surface the match to the user via a merge prompt (see below) before anything is saved.
- On no match: import proceeds normally — save the new `SpendTransaction` with `entryMethod = .imported`, `plaidTransactionID` set, and `MerchantRule` auto-tagging applied (see below).

### Merge prompt UX (product-lead decision — see NOTES for why this wasn't punted)
When a dedup match is found, show a single confirmation-style prompt per matched pair (not a batch review screen — batching is likely over-engineering for what should be a rare event at this user's transaction volume, and a batch UI is real added surface for an edge case): "This looks like a transaction you already added: [manual entry's merchant, amount, date]. Keep as one entry?" with two choices:
- **"Merge"** (recommended/default button): the manual entry is updated in place — `entryMethod` becomes `.imported`, `plaidTransactionID` is set to the newly-fetched Plaid transaction's ID, and Plaid's data **wins** for `amount`/`date`/`merchantName` (Plaid's values are the more likely to be accurate/normalized bank data; the user's manual entry was necessarily an approximation entered from memory or a receipt). The transaction's existing `type` (variable/fixed) and `isManualOverride` flag are preserved as-is from the manual entry — merging must not silently flip a transaction the user explicitly tagged. The new Plaid transaction itself is discarded (not saved as a second row).
- **"Keep both"**: the manual entry is left untouched; the Plaid transaction is imported as a normal new `SpendTransaction` (`entryMethod = .imported`). This is the escape hatch for the case where the match is actually a coincidence (e.g. two separate $12 coffee purchases on the same day) — the dedup heuristic is a heuristic, not certain, so the user must be able to override it.
- No "undo" needed for either choice beyond the app's existing edit/delete affordances (adq.3) — if the user picks wrong, they can edit or delete the resulting transaction(s) the same way they would any other.
- Reasoning for a per-match confirmation rather than silent auto-merge: PROJECT_SPEC frames dedup as a trust-preserving mechanic ("double counting breaks the core mechanic's trust") — silently rewriting a user's manual entry without their awareness would itself be a trust problem if the heuristic is ever wrong (e.g. two genuinely separate same-amount purchases at the same merchant same day). A lightweight per-transaction confirmation is proportionate; a side-by-side field-level compare/pick-winner UI (considered and rejected) is over-built for a heuristic that's right the overwhelming majority of the time.

### MerchantRule auto-tagging at import (absorbs adq.4)
For each transaction that proceeds to import (no dedup match, or "Keep both" chosen), before saving: run it through the same `MerchantMatcher.match([MerchantRule], merchantName) -> TransactionType?` function built in adq.3. If a rule matches, set `type` to the rule's type; `isManualOverride` stays `false` (nothing has overridden it yet — this is identical semantics to adq.3's no-match-default case, just via a different entry path). If no rule matches, default `type = .variable`, `isManualOverride = false` — same defaults as manual entry's no-match case.

### Import summary feedback
After an import batch completes (whether triggered by foreground refresh or pull-to-refresh in adq.6.4, or the debug trigger here), a lightweight, non-blocking confirmation of what happened is appropriate (e.g. a brief "3 new transactions" toast/banner, or simply the Transactions list visibly updating) rather than a full summary screen — this is a background sync, not a user-initiated action requiring a report. Exact mechanism (toast vs. just the list updating) is engineer's call; don't over-build a dedicated summary screen.

## Technical approach

- New `Services/Plaid/TransactionImportService.swift` (or similar name, per STANDARDS §4 `*Service` suffix for I/O boundaries) — orchestrates: call `PlaidService` to fetch new transactions since last sync, map Plaid's transaction shape to a plain domain type, run dedup detection, apply `MerchantMatcher`, and either stage a merge prompt or save directly.
- Dedup detection and the merge-resolution logic (given a fetched transaction + the set of existing manual transactions, decide match/no-match, and given a user's merge/keep-both choice, decide what to write) should be a pure, SwiftData-free function/type in `Services/` per STANDARDS §3/§4 (e.g. `TransactionDedupMatcher` — engineer's naming call) — testable without touching `ModelContext` directly, same pattern as adq.3's `MerchantRuleRetagCalculator`.
- Reuses `Services/MerchantMatcher.swift` from adq.3 as-is (confirmed reusable per adq.3's own acceptance criteria: "structured so reservoir-adq.4 can call the match function directly ... without reimplementing it").
- Plaid's transaction-fetch API: use `/transactions/sync` (Plaid's cursor-based incremental sync endpoint) rather than `/transactions/get` (date-range polling) if available under the app's product access — `/transactions/sync` is Plaid's current recommended pattern for polling-based (non-webhook) clients and avoids re-fetching the full history on every refresh. Engineer to confirm this is available under whatever Plaid product tier/plan this app's Sandbox+eventual-Production credentials have access to; flag back if not (falls back to `/transactions/get` with a stored last-synced date).
- Whether a "last synced" cursor/timestamp needs its own persistence (Keychain? `UserDefaults`? a field on the `AppSettings` singleton PROJECT_SPEC already describes for app-wide settings?) is a technical-approach detail for engineer to resolve within PROJECT_SPEC's existing "`AppSettings` singleton or `UserDefaults`, not a SwiftData model" guidance for app-wide settings.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Triggering an import (debug trigger for this story) fetches transactions from Plaid's Sandbox test institution and, for each, either (a) saves it as a new `SpendTransaction` with `entryMethod = .imported` and `plaidTransactionID` set, or (b) surfaces the merge prompt if a dedup match is found, or (c) applies "Keep both" behavior if the user so chooses.
- [ ] Dedup match criteria (same calendar day + exact amount + case-insensitive exact merchant match against existing manual transactions) is implemented as a pure, unit-tested function — covered by unit tests for: exact match, amount mismatch (no match), date outside same-day window (no match), merchant case-difference (still matches), merchant substring-only (no match, per PROJECT_SPEC's no-fuzzy-matching rule).
- [ ] "Merge" choice: manual entry is updated (amount/date/merchant from Plaid win; `type`/`isManualOverride` preserved from the manual entry); the incoming Plaid transaction is not saved as a separate row. Covered by a unit test.
- [ ] "Keep both" choice: manual entry untouched, Plaid transaction saved as a new `SpendTransaction`. Covered by a unit test.
- [ ] Newly-imported transactions with no dedup match are run through `MerchantMatcher` before save: matching rule sets `type` accordingly (`isManualOverride = false`); no match defaults to `type = .variable`, `isManualOverride = false`. Covered by unit tests for both paths (satisfies adq.4's former scope).
- [ ] Merge prompt UI is covered by an XCUITest: seed a manual transaction and a matching Sandbox-fetched transaction, trigger import, verify the prompt appears with the described copy/choices, and verify both the "Merge" and "Keep both" outcomes end-to-end in the Transactions list.
- [ ] Import pipeline logic (dedup matcher, merge resolution, merchant-match application) lives in `Services/` as SwiftUI-free pure functions/types, unit tested to STANDARDS §5's 80% bar; the Plaid network call itself is exempted from the coverage bar (I/O boundary), same reasoning as adq.6.1.
- [ ] README updated — this story adds a real, user-visible import/dedup feature (STANDARDS §6.4 "product features" trigger).
- [ ] Commits scoped `feat(plaid-import)` per STANDARDS §2.
- [ ] reservoir-adq.4 closed as superseded by this story once these acceptance criteria are met (see NOTES).

## Out of scope

- Foreground/pull-to-refresh triggers themselves (adq.6.4) — this story's import pipeline is triggered manually/via debug hook only.
- Batch/summary review screens for imports (see UX section — a lightweight non-blocking confirmation is sufficient, not a dedicated screen).
- Handling `ITEM_LOGIN_REQUIRED` / relink flows if the linked item errors during fetch (adq.6.5).
- Multi-account linked-item support.

## NOTES

- **Decision: adq.4 absorbed into this story, not built separately.** adq.4's remaining scope (apply the adq.3-built `MerchantMatcher` at import time) is a single acceptance criterion's worth of pipeline wiring, not an independently sequenceable feature — it has no meaningful UX or technical surface apart from "call the function that already exists, at the point transactions are saved." Building it as a separate bead would add sequencing overhead without adding a decision point JP needs to weigh in on. adq.4 is being closed as superseded once this story's ACs are met. **This is a backlog-hygiene call, not a product tradeoff — flag if JP wants adq.4 kept open/separate for tracking reasons.**
- **Open question 2 (merge/dedup UX) — now resolved as a product-lead call within this story**, per the "Merge prompt UX" section above (per-match confirmation, Plaid data wins on merge, type/override preserved, no batch screen). This was explicitly flagged by JP as needing a real decision rather than being left to engineer — the reasoning is stated inline in the UX section. If JP disagrees with the "Plaid data wins" default or the per-match-vs-batch call, that's the place to push back before engineer builds it.
notes: RESOLVED 2026-07-13 (JP): confirmed the merge/dedup UX as designed — per-match confirmation (not batch), Plaid's data wins on amount/date/merchant conflicts, isManualOverride preserved, 'keep both' escape hatch retained. No changes needed.

title: Foreground refresh + manual pull-to-refresh triggers
description:
## Scope

Wire the import pipeline built in adq.6.3 to its two real triggers: app-foreground and manual pull-to-refresh, per PROJECT_SPEC.md's locked "no webhooks... refresh by polling on app foreground plus a manual pull-to-refresh, not on a background timer" decision. Depends on adq.6.3 (the import pipeline must exist before it can be triggered by anything other than a debug hook).

Small story — this is trigger wiring, not new import logic.

## Why this shape (engineer feasibility input, 2026-07-13)

`@Environment(\.scenePhase)` observed at the App/root-View level is the idiomatic SwiftUI trigger for foreground detection (cleaner than `NotificationCenter`'s `UIApplication.willEnterForegroundNotification` for this). Pitfall to route around: don't fire the SwiftData-touching import call directly from a `scenePhase` closure on a view without a live `modelContext` in scope — route the trigger through a view/type that actually holds `@Environment(\.modelContext)`, or through `PlaidService`/`TransactionImportService` holding its own context reference, so the import call isn't accidentally issued from a context-less closure.

## UX

- **Foreground refresh**: on transition to `.active` scene phase (app brought to foreground, not first launch), silently kick off the import pipeline in the background — no loading spinner blocking the UI, no interruption if the user is already looking at a screen. If new transactions land (or a merge prompt is needed), they simply appear/surface once ready, same as the app naturally showing up-to-date data. If a merge prompt is needed as a result of a foreground-triggered import, it should surface the same merge-prompt UI as adq.6.3 (not a modal that ambushes the user mid-navigation — reasonable to queue it and show it once the user is on the Transactions tab, or show it wherever they currently are; engineer/product judgment call at build time, not prescribed further here since it's a minor UX detail with no real tradeoff).
- **Manual pull-to-refresh**: standard SwiftUI `.refreshable` on the Transactions list — pull down, native refresh spinner shows while the import pipeline runs, dismisses when done. This is the user-initiated path and should give clear "something is happening" feedback, unlike the silent foreground trigger.
- **Rate/frequency**: no explicit cooldown/throttle required for MVP — foreground triggers naturally can't fire more often than the user backgrounds/foregrounds the app, and pull-to-refresh is user-initiated. If Plaid Sandbox/Production rate limits become a practical issue during testing, that's a follow-up bead, not a speculative guard to build now.

## Technical approach

- Foreground trigger: `.onChange(of: scenePhase)` (or equivalent) at `RootTabView` or `App` level, calling into `TransactionImportService` (adq.6.3) when transitioning to `.active` from `.background`/`.inactive` (not on cold launch — first-launch import, if desired, is a separate consideration not required by this story since Link itself just happened in that case).
- Pull-to-refresh: `.refreshable { }` modifier on `TransactionsView`'s list (adq.3), calling the same `TransactionImportService` entry point — both triggers share one pipeline, no duplicated import logic (STANDARDS §3).
- No new SwiftData schema changes.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Backgrounding and re-foregrounding the app triggers an import pipeline run (verified via a testable seam — e.g. an injectable/mockable trigger point in `TransactionImportService` that a unit or XCUITest can assert was invoked on the scene-phase transition, not just manual observation).
- [ ] Foreground-triggered import does not block or interrupt the current screen; if new transactions arrive they appear in the Transactions list without user action.
- [ ] Pull-to-refresh on the Transactions list triggers the same import pipeline and shows the native refresh-in-progress indicator until it completes. Covered by an XCUITest (seed a Sandbox transaction, pull to refresh, verify it appears).
- [ ] Both triggers call the same underlying `TransactionImportService` entry point — no duplicated trigger-to-import wiring (STANDARDS §3 no-copy-paste check).
- [ ] README updated if this changes any documented behavior beyond adq.6.3's existing README update (likely a short addition, not a new section).
- [ ] Commits scoped `feat(plaid-import)` per STANDARDS §2 (same scope as adq.6.3, since this is the same feature area).

## Out of scope

- Any background-timer-based refresh (explicitly excluded by PROJECT_SPEC — no webhooks, no backend, no background timer).
- Push notifications on new transactions (Phase 2 per PROJECT_SPEC's feature-prioritization table).
- Explicit rate-limit handling/backoff UI (see UX section — revisit only if it becomes a real problem in practice).

title: Plaid Link + Keychain token storage (foundation)
description:
## Scope

Foundation story for Plaid integration: presents Plaid Link (Sandbox environment only — production switching is a separate story, adq.6.2), exchanges the resulting `public_token` for an `access_token` directly from the device (see Technical approach — resolved), and stores that token in Keychain. This story does **not** cover transaction import (adq.6.3), refresh triggers (adq.6.4), or relink/error-recovery UX for an already-linked item (adq.6.5) — it's the minimum slice that gets one account linked and a token safely stored, so the riskiest unknowns (LinkKit integration, token exchange without a backend, OAuth-institution redirect) are proven out first.

Sequenced first among the adq.6 children — everything else in the Plaid breakdown depends on `PlaidService` existing.

## Why this shape (engineer feasibility input, 2026-07-13)

- **LinkKit version**: build against LinkKit 7.x, which has native SwiftUI support (`.plaidLink(isPresented:token:onSuccess:onExit:onEvent:onLoad:errorView:)` view modifiers / `Handler.makePlaidLinkSheet()`) rather than the older `UIViewControllerRepresentable` wrapper pattern. Distribution is SPM (LinkKit's CocoaPods distribution is being deprecated; no Podfile needed, consistent with STANDARDS' implicit SPM-only assumption).
- **Token exchange — RESOLVED 2026-07-13: direct-from-device.** JP picked option (a): `client_id`/`secret` embedded in-app, exchange called straight from the device against `/item/public_token/exchange`, consistent with the app's existing accepted risk posture for other Plaid API keys (personal/sideloaded use, no backend by design per PROJECT_SPEC.md). No proxy/serverless component.
- **Keychain**: no SwiftData/Keychain bridge ships with iOS — a small `KeychainService` wrapping `Security` framework's `SecItemAdd`/`SecItemCopyMatching`/`SecItemUpdate`/`SecItemDelete` is the idiomatic, minimal approach (not a third-party dependency). Use `kSecAttrAccessibleAfterFirstUnlockThisDeviceOnly` (survives background refresh triggers in adq.6.4, not iCloud-synced, not exportable). No keychain-access-group needed (single target, no extension).
- **OAuth institutions — RESOLVED 2026-07-13: in scope.** JP wants MVP to support institutions that require Plaid's webview OAuth redirect (not just Sandbox's default non-OAuth test institutions), so this story includes the redirect handling rather than deferring it.

## UX

- **Entry point**: this story builds the `PlaidService` and Link presentation plumbing but does **not** build its own permanent UI entry point — Settings (adq.7) owns the "Link a bank account" button in the shipped app. For this story, a minimal, clearly-temporary entry point (e.g. a button behind a `#if DEBUG` flag, or a debug-only row) is sufficient to drive and test the flow end to end. Do not build Settings UI here — that's adq.7's scope and would be duplicated work.
- **Link presentation**: standard Plaid Link modal sheet (institution search/select, credential entry — Plaid-hosted UI, not custom-built). No app-side customization beyond passing Plaid's public/sandbox key and requested products (`transactions`). For OAuth institutions, Link hands off to a webview redirect (registered URL scheme reopens the app) before returning success/exit — this is Plaid's standard OAuth handoff, not custom-built.
- **Success**: on `onSuccess(LinkSuccess)`, extract `publicToken` and the linked institution's display name from `metadata`; proceed to token exchange (see Technical approach). While exchange is in flight, show a lightweight inline spinner/progress state — this is a network call and must not appear to hang with no feedback.
- **Exit without success** (`onExit(LinkExit)` with no error — e.g. user backed out of Link): treat as a silent cancel, return to the entry point with no error UI. This is a normal, expected path (user changed their mind), not a failure.
- **Exit with error** (`onExit(LinkExit)` carrying a `PlaidError`) — RESOLVED 2026-07-13: differentiate error types rather than one generic message. Classify into:
  - **Network error** (no connectivity / request timed out reaching Plaid): "Couldn't reach the network. Check your connection and try again."
  - **Plaid-side error** (Plaid's servers returned an error, institution-level issue, etc.): "Couldn't connect to your bank. Try again."
  - **User-cancelled** (already covered above as a silent, non-error exit): no message.
  In all error cases, do not surface Plaid's raw `errorCode`/`errorType` strings to the user (target user is not financially/technically sophisticated per product framing) — log the raw error for debugging (`os_log`/console), but keep on-screen copy in the two buckets above. A single "Try again" affordance re-opens Link; no multi-step retry/diagnostic flow in this story. This classification is the same one adq.6.5 references for import-time errors — keep the classifier reusable rather than duplicating it.
- **Token exchange failure** (Link succeeded, but the subsequent exchange call fails — network error, malformed response): classify the same way as above (network vs. Plaid-side) and show the matching message. Do not partially persist anything (no orphaned institution record with no token).

## Technical approach

- New `Services/Plaid/` folder (STANDARDS §4):
  - `PlaidService.swift` — protocol owning the Link session lifecycle and token exchange, per STANDARDS' explicit Plaid isolation rule. Exposes an app-domain-shaped API (e.g. `startLink() async`, a published/observable state for presentation, `exchangePublicToken(_:) async throws -> LinkedItem`) — no `LinkKit` types leak past this boundary. Views and any calculator/business-logic code never `import LinkKit` directly.
  - `PlaidServiceLive.swift` (or similar) — the real implementation calling LinkKit + Plaid's REST exchange endpoint directly from the device (`client_id`/`secret` embedded via xcconfig, same handling as other Plaid credentials — not committed, injected at build time).
  - `KeychainService.swift` — small wrapper around `Security` framework calls (`save`, `read`, `delete` for a token keyed by a fixed identifier — single item, single account for now; adq.6's multi-account note is out of scope, see Out of scope).
  - `PlaidErrorClassifier` (or similar) — pure function mapping a `PlaidError`/exchange failure into `{network, plaidSide}` (cancel is handled separately, not a classifier case). Unit tested; reused by adq.6.5 for import-time error classification.
- New domain type (plain struct, no SwiftData): `LinkedItem` (or similar) — institution name, item ID, whatever minimal metadata the rest of the app needs. Storage note: **does not require a SwiftData schema change for this story** — this story only needs to prove Link + exchange + Keychain storage work end to end; where linked-item metadata is queried from the app for real UI (Settings' account list) is adq.7/adq.6.3's concern and may require a schema decision at that point (flag forward, don't resolve here).
- Sandbox-only for this story: hardcode/config Plaid `client_id` + Sandbox `secret` via local, gitignored xcconfig (not committed) — full Sandbox/Production environment switching is adq.6.2; this story just needs Sandbox working.
- OAuth redirect handling: register the URL scheme LinkKit requires for OAuth institution redirects (per Plaid's standard iOS OAuth setup — `Info.plist` URL type + `LinkKit` OAuth redirect URI configuration); no custom webview code, this is SDK-handled.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `PlaidService` protocol exists in `Services/Plaid/`, has no `LinkKit` types in its public surface, and is the sole point where `import LinkKit` appears in the codebase (aside from the Link-presentation view itself, which is unavoidably coupled to the SDK's presentation API).
- [ ] Tapping the (temporary/debug) entry point presents Plaid Link in Sandbox mode using LinkKit 7.x's SwiftUI presentation.
- [ ] Selecting a Sandbox test institution and completing Sandbox test credentials (`user_good`/`pass_good`) succeeds, exchanges the resulting `public_token` for an `access_token` via a direct device-to-Plaid REST call, and stores the `access_token` in Keychain via `KeychainService` — verified by reading it back (e.g. a debug-only "verify token stored" check, or a unit test against `KeychainService` directly with a test token).
- [ ] Selecting a Sandbox OAuth test institution completes the webview redirect handoff and returns to the app via the registered URL scheme, then proceeds through the normal success/exchange path.
- [ ] Backing out of Link before completing (cancel) returns to the entry point with no error message shown.
- [ ] A network-classified error and a Plaid-side-classified error (simulate via Sandbox's error-simulation institutions, e.g. `ins_20`, and a forced offline/timeout condition) each show their distinct message from the UX section above, both offering "Try again," which re-opens Link.
- [ ] A forced token-exchange failure (e.g. mock/stub the exchange call to fail) shows the matching classified error message and leaves no orphaned Keychain entry or partial state.
- [ ] `PlaidErrorClassifier` is unit tested against both classification buckets.
- [ ] `KeychainService` (save/read/delete) is unit tested directly (no LinkKit/network dependency needed for these tests) to STANDARDS §5's 80% bar.
- [ ] `PlaidService`'s pure/mappable logic (e.g. mapping `LinkSuccess`/`LinkExit` metadata into app domain types) is unit tested; the LinkKit SDK call itself and the network exchange call are integration-level and covered by manual verification + notes in the PR, not unit tests (SDK/network boundaries are exempted from the 80% bar by nature, consistent with how `PlaidService` is designed to isolate them).
- [ ] README updated (STANDARDS §5/§6) — this story introduces a new architectural boundary (`Services/Plaid/`) and a new credential-storage mechanism (Keychain), both explicit README-update triggers.
- [ ] Commits scoped `feat(plaid)` per STANDARDS §2.

## Out of scope

- Full Settings UI entry point for linking (adq.7).
- Sandbox/Production environment switching (adq.6.2) — Sandbox only here.
- Transaction import itself (adq.6.3).
- Foreground/pull-to-refresh triggers (adq.6.4).
- Relink / update-mode flow for an already-linked item that starts erroring (adq.6.5) — that story reuses `PlaidErrorClassifier` from here for `ITEM_LOGIN_REQUIRED` specifically.
- Multi-account/multi-item linking (one Keychain entry, one linked item, is sufficient to prove the flow — the data-model question of storing multiple linked items is deferred to whichever story first needs more than one).

## Decisions log (resolved 2026-07-13)

1. **Token exchange approach**: direct-from-device, embedded `client_id`/`secret`. No proxy/backend.
2. **Debug-only Link entry point placement**: any placement fine, gate behind `#if DEBUG`, remove before adq.7 ships the real one.
3. **Error/retry UX**: differentiate network vs. Plaid-side errors (not a single generic message); classifier shared with adq.6.5.
4. **OAuth institutions**: in scope for MVP — Link's standard webview redirect handoff, no custom code beyond URL scheme registration.
notes:
Update after coordinator unstuck CoreSimulatorService: independently re-verified against the REAL remote LinkKit SPM package (project.yml unchanged, no vendor workaround needed) — xcodebuild build succeeded, full ReservoirTests suite (208/208) passed. Confirmed RootTabView's earlier SourceKit diagnostics were stale-index noise: RootTabView.swift shows 100% line coverage from the launch/tab tests, no real issue.

Went further: installed and launched the actual app on the booted simulator (iPhone 16 / iOS 18.5), screenshotted the Today tab (renders correctly), then wrote and ran a new XCUITest suite (ReservoirUITests/PlaidDebugLinkUITests.swift, 3 tests) that live-drives the debug Settings entry point: renders plaidDebug.linkButton, taps it (triggers a REAL network call to sandbox.plaid.com's /link/token/create with the placeholder Config/Plaid.xcconfig credentials, confirmed via curl to return HTTP 400 invalid_field), and asserts the UI shows the exact classified plaidSide message ('Couldn't connect to your bank. Try again.') plus a working Try Again button — genuine end-to-end verification of the network round-trip, PlaidErrorClassifier, and SwiftUI state wiring, not just unit-level. Also verified 'Verify token stored' correctly reports no token when nothing's linked. All 3 pass live; full suite (208 unit + 36 UI) passes with 0 failures. Coverage after this pass: KeychainService 92.3%, PlaidErrorClassifier 100%, PlaidDebugLinkView 92.4%, PlaidLinkPresentationView 68.8%, PlaidServiceLive 48.8% (its LinkKit-session/exchange bodies remain the intentionally-exempted integration boundary).

Committed as a second commit (6f66f1f, test(plaid) scope) on top of the original cce72a0 feat(plaid) commit — kept separate rather than amended, both still on feat/plaid-link-foundation.

Still genuinely blocked, not fixable from this end: I do not have real Plaid Sandbox client_id/secret, so the AC items requiring an actual completed Sandbox Link session (institution search + user_good/pass_good, an OAuth institution's webview redirect handoff, ins_20 error-simulation institution, a real access_token exchange + Keychain readback of a real token, cancelling a live Link sheet) remain unverified beyond code review + the state-machine unit tests (handleLinkExit, handleLinkSuccess's rollback-free design). Needs JP to supply real Sandbox credentials via Config/Plaid.xcconfig for a final manual pass, or to run that pass himself.
