The new second commit (6f66f1f) has NOT been pushed yet, pending that check-in.

title: Verify currentBalance formula sign for goal progress (sums attributed spend transactions additively)
description: Engineer flagged during reservoir-adq.5 implementation: GoalsScreenCalculator.currentBalance implements the bead's literal spec: currentBalance = startingBalance + sum(all SpendTransactions attributed to this goal). Since SpendTransaction.amount is always a positive spend magnitude and there is no 'deposit'/contribution transaction kind in this app, this means MORE spending on a goal's attributed transactions moves the progress bar UP toward targetAmount — the opposite of the app's core 'underspend banks savings, carry-forward' mechanic used everywhere else (daily limit, Today screen). Implemented exactly as the JP-approved bead spec states (with a code comment flagging it), not silently changed, but worth a second look before this ships: does 'attributed transaction' for goal progress mean something different from the spending SpendTransaction used in daily-limit math (e.g. a future separate contribution-tracking concept), or is the formula sign actually backwards and should be currentBalance = startingBalance + banked-surplus-style accounting instead? Needs JP/product-lead decision before this ships to avoid a confusing progress bar that moves the wrong direction.
notes: JP decision (2026-07-10), confirmed with precise walkthrough: goal progress = startingBalance + carryForward (banked-surplus model), matching the app's core daily-limit mechanic exactly. Confirmed operational semantics: spending $0/day banks the full dailyBase toward the goal each day (100% of target reached by targetDate if never spent); spending exactly today's displayed daily limit (dailyBase + carryForward) nets ZERO progress that day (neutral/on-pace but no gain); spending over the limit erodes progress (carryForward goes negative). JP explicitly confirmed this is the intended model ('100% that's exactly what I was thinking'). Implementation: GoalsScreenCalculator.currentBalance should become startingBalance + carryForward (via DailyLimitCalculator/GoalCarryForwardInput, as of referenceDate) — replacing the current startingBalance + sum(attributed transactions) formula. Ready for implementation.
close_reason: Fixed in commit ab872f0 (feature/goals-screen, PR #5): currentBalance now computed as startingBalance + carryForward, matching JP's confirmed banked-surplus semantics. Verified personally (not via relay): 119/119 unit tests pass including 5 new tests exercising the exact confirmed walkthrough (zero-spend reaches targetAmount exactly, spending exactly the daily limit nets zero change, overspending erodes below startingBalance, fixed transactions excluded). 2 UI test failures are the pre-existing unrelated reservoir-4w8 flake.

title: Plaid Link integration + transaction import
description: Integrate Plaid iOS LinkKit per docs/PROJECT_SPEC.md: access_token stored in Keychain (never UserDefaults/committed config); Sandbox during dev, Production once linked to real accounts via build config; no webhooks — refresh on app foreground + manual pull-to-refresh. Dedup: before import, check existing manual transactions with same date+amount(±same-day)+merchant, prompt to merge rather than double-count (this is an MVP requirement, not deferred). Highest external risk — build last per recommended build order, after core logic is de-risked.

title: Today screen (SwiftUI)
description:
Implement the Today tab per docs/PROJECT_SPEC.md UX section: date header with settings icon, hero daily-limit number (44px, centered) with '$base + $carried forward' subtext (single combined sum across all active goals, not per-goal breakdown), two-stat row (spent today / remaining, summed across active goals), recent transactions (last 3, sorted by date then creation order; fixed ones muted 'excluded from limit'), single 'Add transaction' primary action (renders as a minimal stub sheet for now — full entry flow is adq.3). Wire to real SwiftData-backed calculator output via a small pure/testable mapping function (SavingsGoal/SpendTransaction -> GoalCarryForwardInput), not inlined in the view.

Goal lifecycle: a goal is "active" when targetDate >= today. When a goal's targetDate passes, show a success banner on Today with an encouraging completion message. Dismissing the banner resets the goal to a true empty/no-goal state (goal is cleared; user must create a new one to see the hero number again) — requires a persisted dismissal field on SavingsGoal (e.g. dismissedAt) since this can't be derived from targetDate alone.

Empty states: no active goal -> prompt to create a goal instead of hero number (stub nav/sheet for now — full creation flow is adq.5/adq.7); no transactions -> empty-state message, $0 spent / full limit remaining; over-limit -> remaining renders as negative/clearly-over state. Handle orphaned transactions (savingsGoal == nil) explicitly in the spent/remaining sums. Recompute on midnight rollover and on foreground resume. Assume USD formatting.

44px hero sizing is a manual/code-review check (STANDARDS §5), not XCUITest-assertable. Cover presence/content/state transitions (empty state, banner, dismiss-and-reset, normal state) with XCUITest per STANDARDS.md §5.
close_reason: Merged via PR #3 (https://github.com/JohnPease/reservoir/pull/3) after all 8 code review findings fixed on feature/today-screen (commit 14579ec).

title: Daily limit / carry-forward calculator
description: Standalone, unit-tested pure Swift function (no SwiftUI/SwiftData import) implementing the core mechanic from docs/PROJECT_SPEC.md: base = (targetAmount - startingBalance) / totalDaysFromStart, fixed at goal creation/edit; continuous carry-forward (overspend borrows from future days, underspend adds); day boundary = device local midnight; when multiple SavingsGoals are active, total daily limit = sum of each goal's independent base + carry-forward (not pooled); fixed-tagged transactions reduce goal balance but are excluded from the limit/carry-forward math. Cover in unit tests: overspend day, underspend day, goal edited mid-stream, multi-day gaps, multiple concurrent goals, fixed vs variable transaction handling. Target >=80% coverage per STANDARDS.md §5.
close_reason: Implemented pure-Swift DailyLimitCalculator + 14 XCTest cases; verified 19/19 tests pass, 98.33% coverage on the new file. Committed as 5f2c58b on feature/daily-limit-carry-forward, pushed to origin.

title: SwiftData models: SavingsGoal, Transaction, MerchantRule
description: Define SwiftData @Model types per docs/PROJECT_SPEC.md data model section: SavingsGoal (targetAmount, targetDate, startDate, startingBalance, derived currentBalance, derived dailyBase fixed at creation/edit), Transaction (amount, date, merchantName, type variable/fixed, entryMethod manual/imported, plaidTransactionID), MerchantRule (merchantName exact case-insensitive match, type). Wrap in a VersionedSchema (SchemaV1) from the start per STANDARDS.md §4. No User entity — settings live in AppSettings/UserDefaults.
close_reason: Merged via PR #1 into develop; SwiftData models, VersionedSchema, and review fixes landed

title: Daily Limit Finance App — MVP
description: Personal iOS app tracking spending against a rolling daily limit derived from savings goals. See docs/PROJECT_SPEC.md for full spec. This epic tracks the MVP build order: data models -> calculator -> Today screen -> transactions/goals screens -> Plaid integration.

title: PlaidDebugLinkUITests invalid-credentials test fails against real Sandbox creds now that redirect_uri is a valid https universal link
description:
While finishing reservoir-adq.6.1's universal-link OAuth redirect fix (feat/plaid-link-foundation), xcodebuild test showed 2 failures:

PlaidDebugLinkUITests.testTappingLinkButtonWithInvalidCredentialsSurfacesClassifiedError()

This test taps the debug Link button and expects Plaid's real Sandbox API to reject
the configured client_id/secret, producing a .plaidSide classified error. Locally,
Config/Plaid.xcconfig has real (non-placeholder) Sandbox credentials filled in.

Previously this test passed "by accident": the old custom-scheme redirect_uri
(com.johnpease.reservoir.plaid://oauth) was rejected by Plaid's /link/token/create
call regardless of credential validity, since Plaid's dashboard/API now requires an
https redirect URI. That API-level rejection produced the expected error path.

Now that PlaidServiceLive uses the https universal-link redirect_uri
(https://johnpease.github.io/oauth), token creation actually succeeds with real
credentials, Link opens for real (phone-number OAuth entry screen appears), and the
test's assumption (any tap on this button surfaces a classified error) no longer holds.

Not fixed as part of reservoir-adq.6.1's universal-link wiring — out of scope for that
change, and the right fix depends on a call the tester/product-lead should make:
either (a) stub/mock the token-creation network call so this test doesn't depend on
real Sandbox credentials being invalid, or (b) point it at deliberately-invalid
credentials via a separate test-only xcconfig, or (c) restructure what this specific
test is verifying now that the debug entry point can actually succeed locally.

Confirmed via: xcodebuild test -scheme Reservoir -destination 'platform=iOS Simulator,id=13473BCC-9969-45B4-B850-DDE67CB0A049' on 2026-07-14.

title: Item relink / update-mode + connection-status UX
description:
## Scope

Handle the case where an already-linked Plaid item stops working — most commonly `ITEM_LOGIN_REQUIRED` (bank requires re-authentication, e.g. password change, MFA re-verification) — surfaced during a foreground/pull-to-refresh import attempt (adq.6.4), and give the user a way to relink via Plaid's "update mode" Link flow. Depends on adq.6.1 (`PlaidService`) and adq.6.4 (needs a working refresh trigger to actually hit this error state in practice, though the error-handling logic itself could technically be built against adq.6.3 alone).

Flagged explicitly by engineer as a real gap in a webhook-free, no-backend polling design: without webhooks, the app only discovers `ITEM_LOGIN_REQUIRED` (or other item-level errors) the next time it tries to fetch, so the UI needs an explicit "your bank connection needs attention" state rather than assuming errors are rare/transient.

## UX

- **Where this surfaces**: this story's error state needs a persistent home, not just a one-time toast — a broken bank connection stays broken until the user acts, so a transient error message that disappears would leave the user unaware anything's wrong. Settings (adq.7) is the natural home for the linked-account status (it already owns "linked accounts management" per PROJECT_SPEC's IA), so this story adds a status indicator there: a linked account's row shows a "Needs attention — tap to reconnect" state (in place of / alongside its normal display) whenever the last fetch attempt returned an item-level auth error. This story builds the status flag/detection; whether adq.7 exists yet when this is built determines whether this lands as a small addition to adq.7's UI or a minimal standalone status view — sequencing/UI-host detail for engineer, not a product decision.
- **Also surface on Today**, since Settings is not the launch screen and a user might not think to check it: a small, non-blocking banner or icon-badge (exact treatment is a minor UX detail, engineer/product judgment at build time — doesn't need to compete with the hero daily-limit number) indicating a bank connection needs attention, tappable through to the reconnect flow. This keeps the "the number is the hero, but trust in the system matters" principle from PROJECT_SPEC's Today-screen design principles intact — an unnoticed broken connection silently going stale would itself erode that trust.
- **Reconnect flow**: tapping "reconnect" opens Plaid Link in **update mode** (Plaid's mechanism for re-authenticating an existing item without creating a duplicate item/token) for the specific linked item. Same generic-error-message posture as adq.6.1 if update-mode Link itself fails or is abandoned.
- **Successful reconnect**: clears the "needs attention" state; a subsequent refresh (foreground or pull) resumes importing normally. No special one-time "catch-up" import UI needed beyond the normal import pipeline picking up from wherever its cursor/sync-state left off (adq.6.3's technical approach).

## Technical approach

- Extend `PlaidService` (adq.6.1) with an update-mode Link entry point (`startRelink(for: LinkedItem) async` or similar) — Plaid's update mode requires generating a `link_token` scoped to the existing item (via the same token-exchange-adjacent call pattern established in adq.6.1; exact request shape depends on adq.6.1's resolved token-exchange approach, open question 1).
- `TransactionImportService` (adq.6.3) surfaces item-level errors (`ITEM_LOGIN_REQUIRED` specifically, and reasonably any other item-level auth error Plaid returns) distinctly from transient/network errors — a transient network failure during a foreground refresh should **not** trigger the persistent "needs attention" UI (that would be a false alarm on every flaky connection); only a genuine item-level auth error should. This distinction should be a pure, testable classification function, not inlined error-string matching in the view layer.
- Persisted "needs attention" flag: likely lives alongside whatever adq.6.1/adq.6.3 already decided for linked-item metadata storage (flagged forward in adq.6.1 as not-yet-resolved) — resolve as part of that same storage decision, not a second parallel mechanism.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] An item-level auth error (`ITEM_LOGIN_REQUIRED`) returned during an import attempt is classified distinctly from a transient/network error by a pure, unit-tested function — covered by unit tests for both classifications.
- [ ] A genuine item-level auth error sets a persistent "needs attention" state, visible on both the linked-account's row (Settings/adq.7 host) and as a non-blocking indicator on the Today screen, until resolved.
- [ ] A transient/network error during import does **not** set the "needs attention" state (verified by a unit test on the classifier, plus at minimum a manual verification note that a simulated network failure doesn't trip the persistent UI).
- [ ] Tapping the reconnect affordance (from either surface) opens Plaid Link in update mode for the affected item.
- [ ] Successfully completing update-mode Link clears the "needs attention" state; the next refresh resumes normal import. Covered by an XCUITest against Sandbox (Plaid Sandbox supports simulating `ITEM_LOGIN_REQUIRED` for test items — engineer to confirm exact Sandbox mechanism at build time).
- [ ] README updated if this introduces new user-facing states not yet documented.
- [ ] Commits scoped `feat(plaid)` or `feat(plaid-import)` per STANDARDS §2 (engineer's call based on which area the bulk of the change touches).

## Out of scope

- Automatic/silent retry of a failed item-level auth (Plaid does not support silently re-authenticating past MFA/password changes — user action via update-mode Link is required by Plaid's own model, not an app design choice).
- General network-error retry/backoff policy beyond "don't falsely flag needs-attention" (a broader retry policy, if wanted, is a separate concern from this story's item-error-specific scope).
- Multi-account: if/when multi-item linking exists, this story's per-item status model should already generalize, but building/testing multiple simultaneous broken items is not required for MVP (single linked account is the expected MVP usage).

## NOTES — open question for JP

3. **Error/retry UX policy, partially resolved here, partially still open.** This story resolves the specific `ITEM_LOGIN_REQUIRED` / update-mode case with a persistent Settings+Today indicator, since that's a real, expected occurrence (banks periodically require re-auth) and Plaid's own model requires user action via update-mode Link — there's no silent alternative. **Still open**: how aggressively should the app retry/surface *other*, less-defined failure modes (e.g. Plaid's servers erroring, a Sandbox-only test-institution quirk, a malformed response) — is a single generic "try again" (as adq.6.1 already does for Link-time errors) sufficient for those, or does JP want a more general retry/backoff policy across all Plaid-related failures? This story treats only the well-defined `ITEM_LOGIN_REQUIRED` case as needing persistent UI; everything else falls back to adq.6.1's existing generic-error-plus-retry pattern. Flag if that's insufficient.
notes: RESOLVED 2026-07-13 (JP): for failure modes beyond ITEM_LOGIN_REQUIRED, differentiate error types (network vs. Plaid-side) rather than a single generic message — reuse the PlaidErrorClassifier introduced in adq.6.1 rather than building a separate classifier here.

title: Sandbox/Production environment switching (in-app toggle)
description:
## Scope

Sandbox vs Production Plaid environment, switched via an **in-app settings toggle/flag** (not a build configuration) per PROJECT_SPEC.md's decision (amended 2026-07-13 — see Decisions log). Depends on adq.6.1 (`PlaidService` must exist before it can be made environment-aware). Likely lands as part of Settings (adq.7) since that's the natural UI host for the toggle, or as a minimal standalone control if built before adq.7 exists — sequencing/UI-host detail for engineer.

This story is small and mechanical relative to the other adq.6 children — it's about wiring, not new product surface.

## Why this shape (engineer feasibility input, 2026-07-13; environment-switch mechanism amended 2026-07-13)

Both Sandbox and Production credential sets (`client_id`/`secret` pairs) are bundled into the app via a single gitignored xcconfig (not committed), same secret-handling bar as before. At runtime, `PlaidService` reads a persisted flag (e.g. `UserDefaults` or a small settings store — not Keychain, this isn't a secret, just a mode selector) to decide which credential set / base URL to use. Flipping the flag takes effect without a rebuild.

## Technical approach

- `Config/Plaid.xcconfig` (or similar), gitignored, with a checked-in `.xcconfig.example` template documenting both credential sets (`PLAID_SANDBOX_CLIENT_ID`, `PLAID_SANDBOX_SECRET`, `PLAID_PRODUCTION_CLIENT_ID`, `PLAID_PRODUCTION_SECRET`) so a fresh clone knows what to fill in — this satisfies STANDARDS' "environment setup" README requirement (§6.3) alongside the README update below.
- A small `PlaidEnvironment` (enum: `.sandbox` / `.production`) persisted via a settings store, defaulting to `.sandbox`. `PlaidService`'s live implementation reads the current `PlaidEnvironment` at call time (not just once at launch, since it can now change mid-session) to pick the base URL / credential pair — this story removes adq.6.1's temporary Sandbox-only hardcoding.
- Toggle UI: a Settings row (adq.7's host, or a minimal debug-adjacent control if this ships before adq.7) to flip between Sandbox and Production. Given the real-money blast radius of an accidental flip, require an explicit confirmation step (e.g. a confirmation alert: "Switch to Production? This will use real bank data.") rather than a bare switch — this isn't specified by product-lead but is a reasonable safety rail for a single-tap toggle controlling which credential set talks to a real bank; flag to JP if a lighter-weight toggle is preferred instead.
- No SwiftData/data-model changes.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Flipping the in-app toggle from Sandbox to Production (and back) changes which environment `PlaidService` resolves to on the next Link/import call, with no rebuild required — verified by a unit test against the environment-resolution logic with the flag set both ways.
- [ ] No Plaid secret (Sandbox or Production `client_id`/`secret`) is committed to git — the xcconfig file carrying real values is gitignored; a checked-in `.xcconfig.example` documents the required keys with placeholder values.
- [ ] Switching to Production requires an explicit confirmation step, not a bare single-tap toggle (see Technical approach).
- [ ] README updated: environment setup section (STANDARDS §6.3) documents the xcconfig-based credential setup a fresh clone needs (which file to create, which keys, where to get Sandbox vs Production credentials from the Plaid dashboard) and the in-app toggle's location/behavior.
- [ ] Commits scoped `feat(plaid)` per STANDARDS §2 (this now reads as new functionality — a runtime toggle — not pure config plumbing).

## Out of scope

- Actually obtaining/using real Production credentials — that only happens once JP links a real account. This story just builds the switch; flipping it for real is a later, JP-driven decision.
- Any additional environment-status indicator beyond the toggle's own state (e.g. a persistent "SANDBOX" badge elsewhere in the app) — optional follow-up if JP wants it, not assumed here.

## Decisions log (resolved 2026-07-13)

1. **Environment-switch mechanism**: PROJECT_SPEC.md originally locked this as build-configuration-only ("not a runtime toggle"). JP overrode this in favor of an in-app toggle/flag; PROJECT_SPEC.md has been amended accordingly (see `docs/PROJECT_SPEC.md` line ~12-14). Reason not captured beyond JP's explicit choice — flag if a rationale should be recorded.
2. **Operational process for flipping to Production**: resolved by the mechanism decision above — since it's now a runtime toggle rather than a rebuild, JP flips it in-app whenever he's ready to link a real account, gated by the confirmation step in Acceptance criteria. No separate process needed.

title: Verify goal-met (not just target-date-passed) before showing success banner copy
acceptance_criteria:
Unit tests (DailyLimitCalculatorTests, add to existing file):
- isGoalMet == true when spend was under dailyBase every day (clean underspend).
- isGoalMet == true when one or more days went over dailyBase but the goal recovered by targetDate (cumulative >= 0 at end) — proves a bad day alone does not disqualify.
- isGoalMet == false when cumulative carry-forward at targetDate is negative, even if the most recent day(s) were under budget (proves it's the end-state, not the last day, that decides).
- isGoalMet == false when the user overspent only on the final day, tipping a previously-positive cumulative balance negative.
- isGoalMet with no spend entries at all (edge case: dailyBase * totalDays, i.e. treated as full underspend) returns true.
- Boundary case: cumulative balance exactly 0 at targetDate returns true (">= 0", not "> 0").

Unit tests (TodayScreenCalculatorTests, add to existing file):
- isGoalMet(_:) wrapper correctly maps a SavingsGoal + its transactions through to the same result as the DailyLimitCalculator test above, for at least one met and one not-met fixture.

Manual/UI check (TodayView):
- Construct a completed-undismissed goal that nets positive lifetime carry-forward; confirm the "You reached your goal — nice work!" banner renders.
- Construct a completed-undismissed goal that nets negative lifetime carry-forward; confirm the "Your target date has arrived" / "You spent more than planned along the way." banner renders instead, with neutral (not red/alarm) styling — this is a past event being reported factually, not an active warning state.
- Confirm dismiss behavior is unchanged for both variants.

Definition of done: both DailyLimitCalculator.isGoalMet and TodayScreenCalculator.isGoalMet exist and are covered by the tests above; TodayView.CompletionBannerView shows the two distinct copy variants keyed off isGoalMet instead of the current single softened message; no change to active-goal remaining/limit display.
notes:
FLAG FOR JP — please confirm before engineer builds:

1. Priority: currently P2. This is cosmetic (banner copy accuracy), not functional — it doesn't affect the daily limit, carry-forward math, or any number the user relies on day-to-day. It only affects what a dismissible one-time completion banner says after a goal's target date passes. I think P2 is defensible but this could reasonably drop to P3 if the backlog is under pressure; it's not blocking anything. Your call.

2. Definition of "goal met" — this is the part that actually matters for correctness perception: I landed on an END-STATE check (cumulative carry-forward balance >= 0 at targetDate), NOT a "never had a single over-limit day" check. Reasoning: carry-forward exists specifically so a bad day can be paid back later — that's the mechanic working as intended, not a failure. If we disqualified "met" status the moment any single day went over, that would contradict the whole premise of carry-forward and would likely feel wrong/punitive to you when you check the app. Please confirm this matches your mental model of "did I hit my goal" before this gets built — if you actually want a stricter "never went over, even for a day" definition, that's a different (and larger) calculation and changes the story.
close_reason: Merged via PR #4 (https://github.com/JohnPease/reservoir/pull/4). Goal-met verification implemented: DailyLimitCalculator.isGoalMet (end-state cumulative carry-forward check) + TodayScreenCalculator.isGoalMet wrapper + two-state CompletionBannerView copy. 63/63 tests passing, 100% coverage on new functions. Code-reviewed clean.

title: Settings screen (linked accounts, balances, goal management)
description: Settings tab: linked accounts management (Plaid Link entry point, relink/unlink), starting balances entry (onboarding also uses this), goal management (create/edit SavingsGoal, list active goals for multi-goal support).
notes: Flagged during reservoir-adq.5's scoping/sanity pass (2026-07-08): this bead's description still lists 'goal management (create/edit SavingsGoal, list active goals)' as in scope, but adq.5 now owns full goal CRUD (create/edit/delete/list) directly on the Goals tab. Since adq.7 depends on adq.5, the likely intent is Settings should link into adq.5's flows rather than reimplement them — but this bead's description hasn't been updated to reflect that. Needs a scope pass before adq.7 is picked up, not resolved here.

title: MerchantRule auto-tagging
description:
**Narrowed scope (2026-07-07 reconciliation):** originally scoped to cover all MerchantRule auto-tagging, including retroactive re-application to existing transactions. JP has since decided retroactive re-tagging happens synchronously whenever a MerchantRule is created or edited — that behavior, and the merchant-match engine it depends on, is now built as part of reservoir-adq.3 (see that bead's "Scope note" and acceptance criteria), since adq.3's own MerchantRule CRUD UI needs it immediately and it would be wasteful to build it twice.

**What this story owns now**: applying the same merchant-match function at *import time* — when Plaid delivers new transactions (depends on the not-yet-built Plaid integration, reservoir-adq.6), auto-tag each newly-imported `SpendTransaction` using the existing `[MerchantRule]` set before it's saved/shown to the user. A per-transaction manual tag always overrides a MerchantRule match (not applicable to freshly-imported transactions, which start with `isManualOverride == false`, but relevant if a rule later changes and adq.3's retag pass runs against them). No fuzzy/substring matching in MVP — exact, case-insensitive merchant name match only, matching adq.3's semantics.

**Do not rebuild the matching engine.** adq.3 builds and exposes a pure `Services/` function (`[MerchantRule] -> String -> TransactionType?`) for exactly this lookup — this story calls that function at import time rather than re-deriving matching logic. If adq.3's function isn't structured for reuse from this feature area when this story starts, that's a defect in adq.3, not a reason to duplicate it here — flag back to product-lead rather than reimplementing.

Depends on reservoir-adq.3 (for the shared matching function) and, for actual import-time invocation, on reservoir-adq.6 (Plaid Link integration) — this story's UI/data surface is otherwise ready once adq.3 lands, but it can't be meaningfully exercised end-to-end until adq.6 exists. Engineer/product-lead judgment call at planning time on whether to sequence this strictly after adq.6 or build the import-time hook in parallel against a stub import source.
close_reason: Superseded by reservoir-adq.6.3 — MerchantRule auto-tagging scope is now covered by that story's import-time dedup/merge work. Confirmed by JP 2026-07-13.

title: Goals screen (progress + pace projection)
description:
Goals tab: progress and "at current pace" projections per SavingsGoal, derived from the calculator's dailyBase/carry-forward output. Goal creation/edit flow enforces validation: targetDate after startDate and after today at creation time; startingBalance >= 0. All five open product questions from the original spec are now decided (see comments) and folded in below.

## Screen contents

Goals tab shows three sections, in this order, each collapsible/hidden when empty:
1. **Active goals** (isActive per TodayScreenCalculator — targetDate >= today, dismissedAt == nil). Sorted by targetDate ascending (soonest first), matching the Today screen's @Query sort — one sort convention across the app.
2. **Completed, awaiting dismissal** (completedUndismissedGoals — same set that shows a Today-screen banner). Rendered with the same "reached its target date" framing as Today's CompletionBannerView, plus a dismiss action that does the same dismissedAt = .now write TodayView.dismiss(_:) already does — reuse that logic (extract to TodayScreenCalculator or a shared spot, don't duplicate the SwiftData save/rollback pattern per STANDARDS §3).
3. **Dismissed goals**: vanish entirely — CONFIRMED (JP decision #4). Not shown anywhere in this bead; no history/archive view. Matches Today's dismiss flow treating dismissal as terminal.

Empty state: if there are zero goals of any kind, show the same "No active goal yet / Create a savings goal" prompt pattern as Today's NoActiveGoalPrompt (reuse the copy/spirit, this is a second entry point to the same empty state — do not invent different copy).

**Multi-goal display: one card per goal — CONFIRMED (JP decision #1).** Each active goal renders as its own independent card with its own progress/pace. No consolidated/combined view.

### Per-goal card, active section

- **Progress bar + percentage**: `(currentBalance - startingBalance) / (targetAmount - startingBalance)`, clamped to [0, 1] for the bar fill (can display negative/over-100% as text even if the bar itself clamps). `currentBalance` for a goal without a linked account (pre-Plaid, MVP) is `startingBalance + sum(all transactions attributed to this goal)`, mirroring how the daily limit already treats transactions as the source of truth. Percentage rendered as e.g. "62% to goal".
- **Target amount and target date**, always shown as plain text (e.g. "Target: $2,000 by Dec 1").
- **Start date**, always shown as plain text alongside target date when it differs from creation date (see "Goal creation flow — Start date" below), e.g. "Started: Jun 23" — so a backdated goal's window is visible, not hidden (PROJECT_SPEC trust principle).
- **"At current pace" projection — TWO toggleable views on every active card (JP decision #2, reverses the prior single-definition scoping).**

  A per-card segmented control with two segments, **"Pace" (default) / "Simulation"**. Per-card, not a global app-wide toggle: each goal already renders as its own independent card (decision #1), so a global toggle would be inconsistent with that pattern — a goal with plenty of spend history and a brand-new goal have different appropriate defaults, and a single global switch would force one choice on both. Toggle state is local UI state (`@State` per card row), not persisted to the model — cheap, no schema change, resets to "Pace" each time the screen is opened, which is fine since "Pace" is the safe, always-available default.

  **Segment 1 — "Pace" (the light, carryForward-sign-based read, unchanged from the original spec):**
  - Compute the goal's current `dailyLimit` (dailyBase + carryForward, via `DailyLimitCalculator.dailyLimit`) as of today.
  - If `carryForward >= 0`: "On pace to meet your goal by <targetDate>".
  - If `carryForward < 0`: "Behind pace — ~N days behind schedule", N = `ceil(abs(carryForward) / dailyBase)`.
  - `dailyBase == 0` guard: show "Pace unavailable" instead of dividing by zero.
  - Reuses existing calculator output as-is — no new simulation loop, cheapest of the two views.

  **Segment 2 — "Simulation" (new, heavier: extrapolates actual recent average daily spend forward). Precise definition, grounded only in data `SpendTransaction` actually has (date, amount, kind, savingsGoal):**
  - **Window**: trailing 14 calendar days before today (today excluded, same convention as `carryForward`'s "up to but excluding referenceDate's day"), or fewer if the goal's `effectiveStartDate` is younger than 14 days — window length = `min(14, daysSince(effectiveStartDate, today))`. **Why 14, not 7**: a 7-day window is skewed by which weekday "today" happens to be (e.g. a goal checked right after a spendy weekend looks artificially bad); 14 days spans two weekly cycles and smooths that out while staying recent enough to reflect current behavior rather than the goal's entire history. This is a product/UX call, not left to engineer to pick.
  - **Per-day net**: for each day in the window, `dailyBase - variableSpendThatDay` (variable-kind transactions attributed to this goal only; fixed-kind excluded, same rule `DailyLimitCalculator.carryForward` already applies). A day with zero variable transactions contributes a full `+dailyBase` — consistent with how `carryForward` already treats gap days, not a special case invented for this view.
  - **avgDailyNet** = sum of per-day nets over the window, divided by the window's day count.
  - **No-history edge case** (goal created today, or genuinely zero variable spend logged in the window — window day count could be 0 if the goal was created today): do NOT compute an average from zero data points (that would silently imply "perfectly on pace," fabricating a read from an absence of data — the same trust problem the light view was designed to avoid). Instead show: **"Not enough spending history yet"** in the Simulation segment, with no numbers. The segmented control itself stays enabled/visible (so the user can see the option exists and check back later) — do not auto-redirect to the Pace segment, which would be a surprising silent switch.
  - **Output, when computable — both a dollar projection and a date projection (both are directly derivable from avgDailyNet, no need to pick one)**:
    - `daysRemaining` = whole calendar days from today to `targetDate`.
    - `projectedSurplusShortfall` = `avgDailyNet * daysRemaining`. Shown as a signed dollar amount: "Simulation: ~$46 ahead of target by Dec 1" (avgDailyNet >= 0) or "~$46 behind target by Dec 1" (avgDailyNet < 0).
    - `projectedCompletionDate`: if `avgDailyNet < 0`, `targetDate + ceil(abs(projectedSurplusShortfall) / dailyBase)` days — "Projected to finish ~5 days late (around Dec 6)". If `avgDailyNet >= 0`, `targetDate - floor(projectedSurplusShortfall / dailyBase)` days, floored at not going earlier than tomorrow — "Projected to finish ~3 days early (around Nov 28)". If `avgDailyNet == 0` exactly, just "Projected to finish on schedule (Dec 1)", no date arithmetic needed.
    - `dailyBase == 0` guard: same as Pace segment, show "Pace unavailable" instead of dividing by zero (applies to both segments identically).
  - This is explicitly a *simulation/extrapolation*, not a guarantee — copy should read "Simulation: ..." (the segment label itself signals this is a projection based on recent behavior, distinct from the Pace segment's more conservative on-schedule read). No further hedging copy needed beyond the segment label; over-hedging every projection with disclaimers would clutter a two-view toggle a non-sophisticated user needs to parse quickly.

### Per-goal card, completed-undismissed section

Same as Today's completion banner content: "Goal target date reached", target amount, dismiss (X) button. No progress bar/pace projection (neither Pace nor Simulation) — the goal is past its target date, pace is moot.

## Goal creation flow

Fields, in a form (reuses StubSheet's sheet-with-NavigationStack shape, replaced with a real form — this bead retires the "Create a Goal" StubSheet, wiring TodayView's isShowingCreateGoal and this tab's own create action to the same real sheet):
- **Target amount** — required, currency input, must be > startingBalance (a target below the starting balance isn't a savings goal; validated inline, not just on submit).
- **Target date** — required, date picker, must be after today (creation-time "today").
- **Starting balance** — required, currency input, >= 0. Defaults to 0, not blank.
- **Start date — NOW USER-EDITABLE (JP decision #5, reverses the prior "fixed to today, non-editable" scoping).** Date picker, defaults to today, editable to a past date ("backdating"):
  - **Bounds**: must be `<= today` (no future-dating — a future start isn't what "backdating" means and adds no value: `targetDate` already must be after both `startDate` and today, so a future `startDate` would just be a slower way to express a normal future-start goal; reject `startDate > today` at validation, inline error "Start date can't be in the future"). Lower bound: **no earlier than 90 days before today**, validated inline ("Start date can't be more than 90 days ago"). Reasoning: this is a single-goal-cycle personal budgeting tool; JP's stated use case is "I started two weeks ago" — 90 days is generous headroom beyond realistic backdating while guarding against a mis-tapped date picker producing a multi-year-old `startDate` that silently wrecks `dailyBase` and the goal's displayed history.
  - **Mechanical consequence on `dailyBase` — confirmed by reading `DailyLimitCalculator.dailyBase`/`totalDaysFromStart`**: `dailyBase = (targetAmount - startingBalance) / totalDaysFromStart(startDate, targetDate)`. Backdating `startDate` *increases* `totalDaysFromStart` (more whole days between the backdated start and the target), which makes `dailyBase` **smaller** — a softer daily number, not a harsher one. (The original bead's framing guessed the opposite; the actual math favors the user, since spreading the same target amount over more days lowers the daily ask.)
  - **Carry-forward interaction — this is the part that needed a real decision, not a guess.** `DailyLimitCalculator.carryForward` walks every calendar day from `effectiveStartDate` to today and credits `+dailyBase` to any day with no matching `spendEntries`. `SpendTransaction.savingsGoal` is a to-one relationship set at transaction-entry time — there is no retroactive-attribution UI in this app (no way to pick "attribute a transaction I logged before this goal existed to this goal"), so days before the goal's *actual creation* genuinely have zero attributable transactions, not zero real spend. If `effectiveStartDate` were simply the backdated `startDate` (as the calculator's existing signature would do unmodified), every backdated day would be credited a full `+dailyBase` with nothing behind it — e.g. backdating 14 days at a $10/day base hands the user an immediate, fabricated $140 "banked surplus" the moment the goal is created. That is a worse trust violation than a harsh number would have been: it's a number shown as real progress that has zero data behind it, directly against PROJECT_SPEC's "shown, not hidden, to build trust" principle.
  - **Decision**: add `createdAt: Date` to `SavingsGoal` (set to `.now` at creation, never user-editable — a genuine data-model addition; see Acceptance Criteria README-trigger note). The plain-value mapping layer (`TodayScreenCalculator`/`GoalsScreenCalculator`) builds `GoalCarryForwardInput.effectiveStartDate` as `lastEditedDate ?? max(startDate, createdAt)`, not `lastEditedDate ?? startDate` alone. `DailyLimitCalculator` itself needs **no code change** — its `effectiveStartDate` parameter already means "where carry-forward starts accruing"; this bead just changes what value the mapping layer feeds it when `startDate` is backdated. Net effect: `dailyBase` honestly reflects the longer, softer window the backdated `startDate` implies, while `carryForward` never accrues surplus (or, symmetrically, could never accrue borrowed deficit either) for days that predate the goal's real existence in the app. A backdated goal's very first day therefore behaves like a same-day goal with a gentler `dailyBase` — no artificial windfall, no artificial shortfall.
  - This is a data-model change (new field) and a calculator-input-mapping change — flagged explicitly because it triggers the README update requirement (STANDARDS §5/§6), and because engineer needs to know `createdAt` is new, not `startDate` repurposed.
- Submit button disabled until all validations pass; validation errors shown inline under the offending field.

On submit: create SavingsGoal with `dailyBase` computed via `DailyLimitCalculator.dailyBase(targetAmount:startingBalance:startDate:targetDate:)` (using the possibly-backdated `startDate`), `createdAt = .now`, `lastEditedDate = nil`, `dismissedAt = nil`, save via modelContext, dismiss sheet. Save failure: same rollback + alert pattern as `TodayView.dismiss(_:)` (STANDARDS §3 reuse, don't duplicate).

## Goal edit flow

Editable fields: **targetAmount**, **targetDate**. NOT editable post-creation: **startingBalance**, **startDate**, **createdAt** (changing any of these after the fact has no defined carry-forward semantics — same reasoning as before, now extended to `createdAt` since it's the new carry-forward floor).

Edit is reached via a per-goal edit affordance on the active-goal card (e.g. pencil icon — engineer's call on exact SwiftUI component, resolved below to an explicit button rather than swipe, matching the delete affordance), opens the same form pre-filled, targetAmount/targetDate fields editable, startingBalance/startDate/createdAt fields shown read-only for context but not editable.

**Edit-flow validation (resolved gap from product-lead sanity pass)**: edited targetAmount/targetDate are validated against the same rules as creation — targetAmount must be > startingBalance (unchanged, read-only), targetDate must be after today-at-edit-time and after startDate (unchanged, read-only). Same inline, field-level validation and submit-disabled-until-valid behavior as creation; not a separate rule set.

**Confirmation requirement**: YES, editing targetAmount or targetDate requires an explicit confirmation step before saving, because it resets the carry-forward baseline. Confirmation copy: "Changing your target will reset today's carry-forward balance. Any amount you're ahead or behind will not carry over. Continue?"

On confirmed save: set `lastEditedDate = .now`, recompute `dailyBase` via `DailyLimitCalculator.dailyBase(...)` using the *original* startingBalance/startDate (unchanged) and the *new* targetAmount/targetDate, save. The mapping layer's `effectiveStartDate = lastEditedDate ?? max(startDate, createdAt)` already picks this up correctly post-edit (`lastEditedDate` wins once set) — no further calculator changes needed.

## Goal deletion — IN SCOPE, CONFIRMED (JP decision #3)

Reached via a per-goal delete action (explicit icon/menu button, e.g. trash icon — resolved by engineer feasibility pass: swipe-to-delete is ruled out because goal cards render in a `ScrollView { VStack { ForEach } }`, not a `List`, so `.swipeActions` isn't available without a layout restructure, and an explicit button matches the app's existing destructive-action convention, i.e. the "X" dismiss button on completed goals).

- **Requires confirmation** (destructive, irreversible): "Delete this goal? Its N attributed transactions will no longer count toward any daily limit, but will not be deleted." (N computed from goal.transactions.count, omit the transaction clause if N == 0).
- **Attributed SpendTransactions are NOT deleted** — the model's relationship is `@Relationship(deleteRule: .nullify, inverse: \SpendTransaction.savingsGoal)`, so deleting a SavingsGoal already nullifies `SpendTransaction.savingsGoal` at the SwiftData layer with no code change needed; transactions become orphaned (savingsGoal == nil), same status TodayScreenCalculator already treats as "always counts toward spentToday regardless of attributedGoals." This is existing, tested behavior — deletion doesn't require new calculator logic, only the UI action + modelContext.delete(goal) + save (with the same rollback-on-failure pattern as dismiss/create).

## Technical approach (product-lead notes for engineer)

- **Reuse, don't duplicate**: `DailyLimitCalculator.dailyBase`, `.dailyLimit`, `.carryForward` already provide everything the Pace segment needs. The Simulation segment's avgDailyNet/projection math is new pure logic (see calculator-placement decision below).
- **`createdAt` addition — RESOLVED by engineer feasibility pass**: new `SavingsGoal.createdAt: Date`, non-optional, default `.now`, set once at init, never mutated. Requires a real **SchemaV3** with a `.lightweight` `MigrationStage` (same pattern as the existing V1→V2 migration in `Reservoir/Models/Migrations/`) — a non-optional stored property added to an existing `@Model` can't be silently absorbed by the current schema iteration. Giving it a default (`= .now`) in the model declaration lets lightweight/inferred migration handle it without a custom stage. **Flagged consequence, acceptable given no released users**: any goal that exists before this migration lands gets `createdAt` backfilled to the migration run's timestamp, which — under `effectiveStartDate = max(startDate, createdAt)` — retroactively zeroes that goal's carry-forward history the moment the app updates. Since this is pre-release/personal use with no existing installed base to protect, this is a one-time, acceptable side effect, not a blocker; noting it explicitly rather than leaving it implicit.
- **Mapping layer**: `effectiveStartDate = lastEditedDate ?? max(startDate, createdAt)` replaces the prior `lastEditedDate ?? startDate` wherever `GoalCarryForwardInput` is constructed from a `SavingsGoal` (currently in `TodayScreenCalculator`, and wherever this bead's Goals-screen calculator builds the same input). **This changes already-shipped, already-tested logic** (PR #3, reservoir-adq.2) — existing `TodayScreenCalculatorTests` covering the old `lastEditedDate ?? startDate` formula must be re-verified/updated against the new formula as part of this bead, not just supplemented with new backdating-specific tests.
- **Calculator placement — RESOLVED by engineer feasibility pass**: new **`GoalsScreenCalculator`**, not an extension of `TodayScreenCalculator`. The Simulation segment's avgDailyNet/projection math is substantial new pure logic with no natural home in a file already scoped to "Today screen: lifecycle + summary." Goal lifecycle primitives (`activeGoals`, `completedUndismissedGoals`, `isGoalMet`, the `GoalCarryForwardInput` mapping) stay in `TodayScreenCalculator` as the single source of truth; `GoalsScreenCalculator` calls into those rather than duplicating them — two SwiftData-aware mapping layers under the same STANDARDS §4 exception, each scoped to the screen that owns its unique math.
- **Dismiss/create/edit/delete save pattern**: extract `TodayView.dismiss(_:)`'s mutate/try-save/rollback/alert shape into something shared rather than four independent copies (STANDARDS §3).
- **StubSheet retirement**: removes `CreateGoalStubSheet` usage from both `TodayView` and the new Goals tab; `StubSheet` itself stays for Add Transaction/Settings stubs.
- **`dailyBase == 0` guard — clarified by product-lead sanity pass**: given creation validation requires `targetAmount > startingBalance` and `targetDate > startDate` (both strict), this guard is not reachable through the validated UI flow — it is a defensive guard inherited from `DailyLimitCalculator`'s existing signature, exercised via direct unit test on the calculator/mapping layer, not a state a tester should expect to reproduce by driving the UI. Do not weaken creation/edit validation trying to make this guard UI-reachable.

## Product decisions log (all five prior open questions, now resolved)

1. **Multi-goal display**: one card per goal — confirmed.
2. **"At current pace" definition**: BOTH the light carryForward-sign read and a heavier 14-day average-spend simulation, toggleable per-card via a segmented control ("Pace" / "Simulation", default "Pace") — see full spec above.
3. **Goal deletion**: in scope — confirmed.
4. **Dismissed-goal history**: goals vanish entirely on dismiss, no history view — confirmed.
5. **Start date editability**: now user-editable at creation (backdating), bounded to `<= today` and `>= today - 90 days`, with a new `SavingsGoal.createdAt` field added specifically so carry-forward accrual floors at the goal's real creation date rather than the user-chosen backdated `startDate` — see full mechanical spec above.

## Engineering questions resolved (2026-07-08 sanity pass, product-lead + engineer)

The three items previously flagged "engineer's call, not deciding unilaterally" are now resolved and folded into the sections above:
- `createdAt` migration: SchemaV3 + lightweight MigrationStage, default `.now`; pre-existing goals backfill to migration timestamp (flagged, acceptable pre-release).
- Calculator placement: new `GoalsScreenCalculator` for goal-specific math (currentBalance, avgDailyNet/simulation); lifecycle primitives stay in `TodayScreenCalculator`, called into rather than duplicated.
- Delete/edit affordance: explicit icon buttons (not swipe) for both — goal cards aren't in a `List`, so `.swipeActions` isn't available without a layout change; matches the app's existing explicit-button convention (Today's "X" dismiss).

Also fixed from product-lead's sanity pass: edit-flow validation was previously unspecified (now explicit — same rules as creation); `dailyBase == 0` guard clarified as defensive/unreachable via validated UI, not a state to hunt for in manual testing; existing `TodayScreenCalculatorTests` must be updated (not just supplemented) for the `effectiveStartDate` formula change, since it touches already-shipped logic from PR #3.

**Separately flagged, not part of this bead's scope**: reservoir-adq.7 (Settings screen)'s own description still lists "goal management (create/edit SavingsGoal...)" as its scope, which is now fully redundant with this bead's full CRUD. adq.7 already depends on adq.5, suggesting Settings should link into these flows rather than reimplement them, but adq.7's description wasn't updated to say so — flagged via a comment on adq.7, not resolved here.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Goals tab lists active goals (sorted by targetDate ascending) and completed-undismissed goals, reusing TodayScreenCalculator.activeGoals/completedUndismissedGoals — no second lifecycle model invented for this screen. Dismissed goals never appear anywhere in this bead (no history view).
- [ ] Zero-goals empty state matches Today's NoActiveGoalPrompt pattern (same copy/spirit), reached from both tabs.
- [ ] Active-goal card is one-card-per-goal (no consolidated multi-goal view) and shows: progress bar (clamped 0-1) + percentage text, target amount, target date, start date, and a per-card segmented control ("Pace" / "Simulation", default "Pace") switching between the two pace-projection reads.
- [ ] "Pace" segment: carryForward-sign-based on-pace/behind-pace text exactly as specified (on pace by <targetDate> if carryForward >= 0; "~N days behind schedule" if < 0, N = ceil(abs(carryForward)/dailyBase)).
- [ ] "Simulation" segment: computes avgDailyNet over a trailing-14-day (or shorter, if goal younger than 14 days) window of variable-kind spend attributed to the goal; outputs both a projected surplus/shortfall dollar amount at targetDate and a projected completion date, per the exact formulas in the description. Zero-data window (goal created today / no spend logged) shows "Not enough spending history yet" instead of fabricating a zero-average read — control stays visible/enabled, no auto-redirect to Pace segment.
- [ ] dailyBase == 0 edge case (same-day start/target) shows "Pace unavailable" in both Pace and Simulation segments instead of dividing by zero or crashing. Unit tests cover this for both segments.
- [ ] Unit tests for Simulation segment cover: window truncation for goals younger than 14 days, the zero-history edge case, avgDailyNet >= 0 (ahead/early) and < 0 (behind/late) branches, and the dollar/date projection formulas.
- [ ] Completed-undismissed goal card matches Today's completion-banner content/copy and dismiss (X) action; no progress bar or pace projection (neither segment) shown. Dismiss reuses the same modelContext.save() + rollback-on-failure + error alert pattern as TodayView.dismiss(_:), not a duplicated implementation (STANDARDS §3).
- [ ] Goal creation form: targetAmount (required, > startingBalance), targetDate (required, after today), startingBalance (required, >= 0, defaults to 0), startDate (required, defaults to today, editable/backdatable, must be <= today and >= today-90 days — both bounds validated inline with the exact error copy in the description). Submit disabled until valid.
- [ ] Goal creation replaces the "Create a Goal" StubSheet: both TodayView's empty-state "Create a goal" button and the Goals tab's own create entry point open the same real sheet (STANDARDS §3, no copy-paste).
- [ ] SavingsGoal gains a new `createdAt: Date` field (non-optional, default `.now`), landed via a new SchemaV3 + lightweight MigrationStage (matching the existing V1→V2 migration pattern), never user-editable and never shown as editable in the edit form. This is a data-model change — README updated per STANDARDS §5/§6 to document the new field and why it exists (carry-forward accrual floor, decoupled from the user-facing, backdatable `startDate`), including a note that pre-existing goals get `createdAt` backfilled to the migration timestamp (acceptable, no released users to protect).
- [ ] Existing `TodayScreenCalculatorTests` covering the pre-backdating `effectiveStartDate = lastEditedDate ?? startDate` formula are reviewed and updated (not just supplemented) to reflect the new `lastEditedDate ?? max(startDate, createdAt)` formula, confirming no regression to already-shipped Today-screen carry-forward behavior.
- [ ] On create: dailyBase computed via DailyLimitCalculator.dailyBase(...) using the (possibly backdated) startDate; createdAt = .now; lastEditedDate = nil; dismissedAt = nil.
- [ ] The SwiftData -> GoalCarryForwardInput mapping (TodayScreenCalculator and/or GoalsScreenCalculator) computes effectiveStartDate as `lastEditedDate ?? max(startDate, createdAt)`, not `lastEditedDate ?? startDate`. Unit/integration test confirms: a goal backdated e.g. 14 days with zero attributed transactions shows carryForward == 0 on creation day (no fabricated windfall), and dailyBase reflects the full backdated-to-target day count (smaller/softer than an equivalent same-day-start goal with the same target/amount).
- [ ] Goal edit form: targetAmount/targetDate editable; startingBalance/startDate/createdAt shown read-only, not editable. Reached via a per-goal edit action (explicit button, e.g. pencil icon — not swipe, per resolved SwiftUI-affordance question).
- [ ] Edited targetAmount/targetDate are validated against the same rules as creation (targetAmount > startingBalance; targetDate > today-at-edit-time and > startDate), inline field-level errors, submit disabled until valid — not left unvalidated.
- [ ] Editing targetAmount or targetDate requires an explicit confirmation dialog before saving, with copy that plainly states carry-forward will reset (exact requirement, not left as an implementation detail).
- [ ] On confirmed edit save: lastEditedDate = .now; dailyBase recomputed via DailyLimitCalculator.dailyBase(...) using unchanged startingBalance/startDate and new targetAmount/targetDate; effectiveStartDate = lastEditedDate ?? max(startDate, createdAt) mapping picks this up (lastEditedDate wins) with no DailyLimitCalculator changes needed.
- [ ] Goal deletion: per-goal delete action (explicit icon/menu button, not swipe — goal cards render in a ScrollView/VStack/ForEach, not a List, so .swipeActions isn't available without a layout change) with a confirmation dialog stating attributed transaction count and that transactions are preserved (orphaned), not deleted. Deletion calls modelContext.delete(goal) + save with the same rollback-on-failure/error-alert pattern as create/dismiss/edit.
- [ ] Deleting a goal orphans its SpendTransactions (savingsGoal == nil) via the existing SchemaV1/V2 .nullify delete rule; a unit or integration test confirms transactions survive goal deletion and still count in TodayScreenCalculator.spentToday as orphaned transactions.
- [ ] Unit tests (business-logic target, 80% coverage per STANDARDS §5) cover: progress percentage math (including >100% and negative cases), Pace segment on-pace/behind-pace branching and N-days-behind formula, Simulation segment per above, dailyBase==0 guard (both segments), createdAt-floored effectiveStartDate mapping (including the backdated-with-no-transactions case), creation validation (all four fields including startDate's two bounds), edit recompute of dailyBase/lastEditedDate.

- [ ] README updated (STANDARDS §5/§6) — this bead adds a real Goals screen, goal CRUD, a new SavingsGoal.createdAt model field, and backdatable startDate — all features/architecture/data-model changes that trigger the README update requirement.
- [ ] Commit scope naming: `goal-progress` (matches STANDARDS §2's example scope) for calculator/projection work; `goals` or similarly-named scope for the screen/CRUD UI work — engineer's call on the exact split, but consistently scoped so commits map to this bead.
